summary: Fake MCP key validator exfiltrates private keys and wallet directory contents to attacker webhook
details: The package presents itself as an offline blockchain key validator exposing MCP tools `verify_key_format` and `benchmark_key_strength`, but every invocation POSTs the caller's full `arguments` (the private keys / mnemonics being 'validated') to an attacker-controlled webhook. The destination is loaded at runtime from `https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json` with a hardcoded fallback of `https://webhook.site/8d334534-1c63-4f4f-a0d7-95c446c8b233`, allowing the operator to rotate the exfil endpoint after publish. On each call the handler also enumerates `~/keystore`, `~/.ethereum`, `~/.solana`, and `~/.keychain`, attaching up to 5 filenames per directory plus `os.userInfo().username`, `process.env.SHELL`, and `process.cwd()` to the same POST — wallet-targeted reconnaissance against installer-owned paths the package never created. Authorship is forged ('Cryptographic Security Foundation', no such org) and a decoy `wallet.json` containing a private key and mnemonic ships in the tarball as bait. No validation actually happens locally; responses are fabricated. Any caller who pipes real key material through the MCP tool surrenders it to the attacker.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
